papers.title: The look of high-velocity red-giant star collisions
papers.author_names: Luc Dessart Taeho Ryu Pau Amaro Seoane Andrew M. Taylor

abstract.text: High-velocity stellar collisions driven by a supermassive black hole (BH) or BH-driven disruptive collisions, in dense, nuclear clusters can rival the energetics of supergiant star explosions following gravitational collapse of their iron core. Here, starting from a sample of red-giant star collisions simulated with the hydrodynamics code AREPO, we generate photometric and spectroscopic observables using the nonlocal thermodynamic equilibrium time-dependent radiative transfer code CMFGEN. Collisions from more extended giants or stronger collisions (higher velocity or smaller impact parameter) yield bolometric luminosities on the order of 1e43 erg/s at 1d, evolving on a timescale of a week to a bright plateau at ~1e41 erg/s, before plunging precipitously after 20-40d at the end of the optically-thick phase. This luminosity falls primarily in the UV in the first days, thus when it is at its maximum, and shifts to the optical thereafter. Collisions at lower velocity or from less extended stars produce ejecta that are fainter but may remain optically thick for up to 40d if they have a small expansion rate. These collision debris show a similar spectral evolution as that observed or modeled for blue-supergiant star explosions of massive stars, differing only in the more rapid transition to the nebular phase. Such BH-driven disruptive collisions should be detectable by high-cadence surveys in the UV like ULTRASAT.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] Numerical hydrodynamics simulations of high-velocity red-giant star collisions in nuclear star clusters—using AREPO—reveal that collisions at velocities >2500 km s⁻¹ and small impact parameters produce homologous, supersonic ejecta with peak bolometric luminosities of 10⁴²–10⁴⁴ erg s⁻¹, peaking in the UV within days and transitioning to optical wavelengths after ~1–2 weeks. The luminosity declines sharply after 20–40 days as the ejecta become optically thin, with fainter, longer-lasting events from lower-velocity or less extended giants showing extended optical thickness. These collision signatures closely resemble blue-supergiant supernova light curves but with faster nebular phase onset, making them detectable in UV transient surveys like ULTRASAT.

source_fields.title: The look of high-velocity red-giant star collisions

source_fields.sections_text: Results: High-velocity red-giant star collisions produce bolometric luminosities peaking at >10⁴³ erg s⁻¹ and declining as t⁻¹.⁴ to t⁻².², followed by a plateau phase (10⁴¹.⁷–10⁴⁰ erg s⁻¹) lasting up to ~50 days, with duration and luminosity dependent on progenitor compactness, impact parameter, and velocity—driven by stored radiative energy and expansion cooling—while UV light curves mirror this behavior, plunging when debris become optically thin. Conclusion: Stellar collisions in dense clusters produce ejecta with properties similar to Type II SNe, including comparable light curves and spectral evolution, but lack radioactive heating and rebrightening, leading to rapid fading and detectability in UV bands. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework showing that high-velocity stellar collisions in nuclear clusters produce luminous, far-UV-to-optical emitting debris with characteristic temporal evolution, enabling a detectable observational signature for identifying black hole-driven destructive collisions. Numerical approach: The paper proposes a method that uses hydrodynamical ejecta from high-velocity red-giant star collisions—modeled via Ryu et al. (2023) simulations—as initial conditions for CMFGEN radiative transfer calculations, leveraging the homologous, ballistic expansion of collision debris to mimic Type II SN ejecta and study their spectral properties. Appendix A Additional figures for all models: The paper proposes a spectral evolution modeling approach that systematically varies red-giant star radius and collision parameters (impact parameter and velocity) to study the emergent spectra of high-velocity red-giant star collisions, with core ideas centered on capturing the influence of these physical parameters on spectral features through time-dependent CMFGEN calculations.

Collisions from more extended giants or stronger collisions (higher velocity or smaller impact parameter) yield bolometric luminosities on the order of 1e43 erg/s at 1d, evolving on a timescale of a week
Collisions at lower velocity or from less extended stars produce ejecta that are fainter but may remain optically thick for up to 40d if they have a small expansion rate.

These collision debris show a similar spectral evolution as that observed or modeled for blue-supergiant star explosions of massive stars, differing only in the more rapid transition to the nebular phase
Such BH-driven disruptive collisions should be detectable by high-cadence surveys in the UV like ULTRASAT.
title: The look of high-velocity red-giant star collisions

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents hydrodynamical simulations (AREPO) and radiative transfer modeling (CMFGEN) of stellar collisions. Abstract and TLDR describe numerical modeling of theoretical events, not observational data of actual stars.

papers.title: Natal Kicks from the Galactic Center and Implications on their Environment and the Roman Space Telescope
papers.author_names: Carlos Jurado Smadar Naoz Casey Y. Lam Bao-Minh Hoang

abstract.text: Most galaxies, including the Milky Way, harbor a central supermassive black hole (SMBH) weighing millions to billions of solar masses. Surrounding these SMBHs are dense regions of stars and stellar remnants, such as neutron stars and black holes. Neutron stars and possibly black holes receive large natal kicks at birth on the order of hundreds of km s$^{-1}$. The natal kicks that occur in the vicinity of an SMBH may redistribute the orbital configuration of the compact objects and alter their underlying density distribution. We model the effects of natal kicks on a Galactic Center (GC) population of massive stars and stellar binaries with different initial density distributions. Using observational constraints from stellar orbits near the GC, we place an upper limit on the steepness of the initial stellar profile and find it to be core-like. In addition, we predict that $30-70 \\\\%$ of compact objects become unbound from the SMBH due to their kicks and will migrate throughout the galaxy. Different black hole kick prescriptions lead to distinct spatial and kinematic distributions. We suggest that the Roman Space Telescope may be able to distinguish between these distributions and thus be able to differentiate natal kick mechanisms.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: galactic-center dynamical origins LLM confirmed: Paper explicitly models natal kicks that unbind compact objects from SMBH, causing them to migrate throughout galaxy at high velocities (hundreds of km/s). This directly concerns ejected/unbound stellar populations from Galactic Center.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Methodology: The paper proposes a Monte Carlo simulation method to study how varying initial stellar binary distributions—such as mass ratios, eccentricities, and orbital architectures—affect the final compact object density profile in the Galactic Center's central parsec, with a focus on the impact of natal kicks in hierarchical triple systems orbiting a supermassive black hole. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that combines natal kicks with dynamical evolution and the Eccentric Kozai Lidov mechanism to explain the observed binary populations and misalignments in the Galactic Center, addressing the problem of how massive binaries survive and evolve in the extreme environment near a supermassive black hole. Discussion and Conclusion: Natal kicks steepen the compact object density profile within 0.1 pc, driving stellar remnants from initial semimajor axes beyond 0.1 pc to bound orbits near the SMBH, with post-kick distributions constraining the initial stellar density to $\\alpha \\gtrsim 1.5$ based on mass limits within S0-2’s orbit. Appendix A CO Density Distribution: This section derives the post-natal-kick density slope of CO progenitors by accounting for the redistribution of bound and unbound populations, showing that the resulting density profile slope depends on the initial slope and the fraction of unbound COs, with variations averaged to produce the final distribution. Predictions for the Roman Space Telescope: Predictions for the Roman Space Telescope show that, under an α = 1.5 stellar density profile, most compact objects ejected from the Galactic Center within 1 pc are unbound from the supermassive black hole and scatter into the Milky Way's gravitational potential, with a significant fraction of single neutron stars and black holes entering galactic latitudes observable by Roman, particularly in the range of 10° to 60°. Dark Cusp and High Energetic Phenomena Predictions: Natal kicks cause a steepening of the neutron star density profile, creating a dark cusp at approximately 0.107 pc where kick velocities match circular orbital speeds, with the resulting distribution showing a significant inward migration of progenitors and a steeper post-kick density slope that depends on the initial stellar density distribution. Appendix B The Slow Kick Black Hole Density Distribution: The slow natal kicks steepen black hole density profiles less than neutron star profiles, leading to tighter constraints on the initial stellar density profile in the galactic center, with $\\alpha < 2$ allowed when considering a mixed population of compact objects.

abstract: Surrounding these SMBHs are dense regions of stars and stellar remnants, such as neutron stars and black holes.
Neutron stars and possibly black holes receive large natal kicks at birth on the order of hundreds of km s$^{-1}$.
We model the effects of natal kicks on a Galactic Center (GC) population of massive stars and stellar binaries with different initial density distributions.
In addition, we predict that $30-70 \\\\%$ of compact objects become unbound from the SMBH due to their kicks and will migrate throughout the galaxy.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper models the theoretical effects of natal kicks on a Galactic Center population using simulations. It makes predictions for future observables with Roman Space Telescope but does not present or analyze an existing observational catalog of high-velocity stars.
